sentences.text: After I got my ration card, and we went to the city hall, just because I spoke very good German, I demand things.
And the German people at that time, they were very-- actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were-- they felt guilty because they knew what they did to us.
And they tried to make it good, or at least to show that they want to make good.
So the mayor, because there was a little small town, and we were, my friend and I, we were the only actually survivors who came to that little town.
So we were novelty.
And because we were a novelty, they tried to give us the best.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
After we were for about a month, after we lived there for a month in that rooming house, we demand a better headquarter-- a better-- better rooms.
And sure enough, they gave us-- they gave us a-- a room in somebody's apartment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was much more comfortable and nicer.
And we felt more of a sudden like human being again.
They gave us a ration card, and they gave us also clothing.
And they gave us some money, too.
They gave us some money to buy the clothing.
We didn't need-- have any money, but we had some money.
And in that little town, after we were liberated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met some American captain.
He was from Brooklyn.
He gave me a little prayer book with his-- and he ins-- he put his name on.
Goldberg.
And after-- actually, after the liberation, after I was liberated, I developed-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] pleurisy.
And I went to a hospital.
He took me to a hospital, the captain.
And they gave me good care.
And I recuperated.
And that was the first couple-- couple weeks, I mean, after the liberation.
And that captain introduced me to a couple American soldiers.
And the American soldiers had a lot of German money because everybody thought that the German money would be-- wouldn't be worth anything.
And they gave us some German money, which to them was nothing because they couldn't buy anything out of it.
It was just like a paper, like paper.
So he says, They gave it to us.
Says you might be able to use it.
And you might-- And we just happened to use that money to buy our self clothes because we got it.
And we've become people all of a sudden.
We become person.
And after we came to our self, we decide to look around.
And we heard that in not far away from the place where we were was a camp with a lot of Jewish people there.
And the camp was Belsen-Bergen.
That was the concentration camp.
Yeah.
And then it be--
That was the Belsen-Bergen.
Become a DP camp, yes.
What it was, they moved from the concentration camp-- right next to the concentration camp was army barracks, was a base, actually, army base.
It was air force or something like that.
I mean, and they moved the people from that concentration camp to those barracks in where the German soldiers used to be, which was comfortable.
And that become a DP camp.
But the name was the same.
It was Belsen-Bergen.
So when we--
Actually in German, you say Belsen-Bergen.
In English, you say Bergen-Belsen, the same that-- but that's the reason why because Bergen was a little town, and Belsen was the next town.
And they was right next to each other, and they called it together Belsen-Bergen.
That's what it was.
But are actually are two little separate towns.
But that's the name.
That's the name what everybody knows it, Belsen-Bergen.
And maybe the concentration camp wasn't Belsen.
And later on, the DP was in Bergen and be-- maybe that was the-- that's what it, I think it was.
So when we found out that there was a-- a group of Jewish people not far, that was like 50 miles away, we managed to hitchhike because everybody was hitchhiking that time, whatever [INAUDIBLE].
And the people used to take out of, what it was, army vehicles or private vehicles, whatever trucks, mostly trucks.
And we used to jump on the trucks, and they used to hitchhike on the trucks.
And we finally wound up in Belsen-Bergen.
And there was a lot of people over there.
And the first thing, what it was, we were looking for somebody which we knew.
And who we came across?
I came across of my future wife.
What was her name?
It was Lola, Lola [?
Gertler. ?] And we were looking for somebody, somebody familiar because somebody from-- from-- from our town or somebody who we had some-- something in common.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found out that she was there.
And I found her.
And we found a few other people which we were together in that-- in-- in Krakow in the prison.
And we met because the fellow who I was with him, he was with me in that prison too.
That's why we were staying together.
We were buddies.
And he met somebody, or also from the-- from that prison, and we become friends because we had something in common.
So I decide ask my wife-- not my future wife at that time.
I mean, I told her, I said that your condition in the DP camp are not so good.
Maybe you want to come to our town where we are, and maybe I can manage for you to get some clothing because we had some in in that particular town.
So she decide to go with me.
And I managed to buy her some clothing because you couldn't buy anything in there.
All the stores were closed.
There was no-- no-- no shopping or something.
You couldn't go in.
Everything was on the ration-- ration cards.
And even the stores, what they had, sell-- that were selling for it on the ration cards didn't have much of anything.
But because I knew somebody in that little town, so I managed to buy her a wardrobe.
And we got a little more friendlier And-- and we decide to get married after awhile.
How long did you know her before you got married?
Oh, well, almost a year before we got married.
The wedding, we decided to get married.
And so we had to make it legal.
And we were going to Frankfurt am Main because we registered already to go.
We found out Lillian-- my wife found out that she's got relatives in the-- in the United State.
And we decide to go-- come to-- to America.
And we registered in Frankfurt am Main to go to America.
Yeah.
United States open up a quota because in order to come to the United States, every country has a quota.
So many-- at-- at that time.
I don't know how it's today, but that's how it was then.
If you were born, let's say, in England, you had a quota.
You were born in Germany, you had a quota.
You were born in Poland, you had a quota.
And was only so many people from that country could come every year to-- to the United States as a immigrant.
But at that time, they open up a DP quota.
So United States decides that they will take in the displaced person, that they will take, I think, 100,000 or 200,000.
And the Congress passed a law that they will be out of the quota, out of the quota, as long as you are displaced person.
And we become displaced person.
So we had to register.
So we registered in Frankfurt am Main for going to-- to America.
Under what country's quota?
As a DP.
Yes.
We went to HIAS, and we went on the--
No, we didn't work.
There was not much work to do.
And we were just [? providing-- ?]
My health was fairly good.
Fairly good.
What-- my-- my wife's relatives helped.
We registered HIAS.
And HIAS contact my wife's relatives.
And they, I think, paid for the--
For the fare.
For the fare, for the-- for the ship.
And that speed it up, I think, maybe our coming.
So within a few months, we got the OK, and we came to United States.
We came originally-- we-- we came to Newark, New Jersey.
In Newark, New Jersey, my first job-- well, we came to our relatives.
And we stayed for a week with the relatives.
And after that, we decide we have to do something.
And they were looking for a job.
And my first job was in a-- a watch maker, jewelry supply house.
I couldn't speak any English, but I-- I got a job as a-- as a-- as a salesman, as a salesperson.
That was very awkward, [LAUGHS] very [INAUDIBLE].
But--
German, mostly.
German, Polish, but German mostly.
Now after a while, I worked as an-- in a supply house for about three months.
And I-- I quit because I got a offer as a painter because I had experience during the war to be a painter.
And we got acquainted with some-- a paint-- paint-- contractor, a painter contractor.
And he offered me a job.
And the job paid almost twice as much what I was getting in the supply house.
And so I took the job as a painter.
And I was doing-- doing better than at the other job, when I was working over there as a painter for six months.
And winter came, and we didn't have any job in winter time because not too many people do painting in the winter time.
And my wife uncle open up a bakery.
So I decided to go to the-- and he offered me a job in the bakery, not that I knew what I was doing.
But he gave me a job there.
And 15 years later, I was still working in the bakery.
I become a baker.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And 15 years later, we were invited to a bar mitzvah, to two-- we got invitation to two bar mitzvahs.
One was in Newark, New Jersey, where we-- where we used to live.
And the other invitation we got to Canada.
And the invitation to bar mitzvah was from a person which we spent war time in Krakow prison because we used to keep in touch, whoever survived, with the few people who survived.
What we went through together, we kept in touch with each other because we felt like-- like a family.
So after getting the invitation to the two bar mitzvahs, I said to my wife, [?
Lily, ?] let's go to Canada.
We've never been to Canada.
Let's go to Canada for the-- and [?
Lily-- ?] and my wife said, Why should we go to Canada when we have a bar mitzvah right here in town?
And I said, No, let's go to Canada.
We never been there.
Let's go to Canada.
And by going to Canada, a change-- we-- we went to-- changed my life in an odd-- in a certain way.

The reason why, being at that bar mitzvah, sitting at the table and reminiscing with all the people at the same table about our war experience and questioning one another, what you did during the war and what the other person did through the war, I asked one person who was there where they spent the war.
And they told me they spent the war in Russia.
And the next question was, I said, did you heard about somebody from my home town?
And my sister was married to a band leader.
He had a orchestra.
And he had a quite famous orchestra.
So I asked them, do you ever heard about the orchestra leader or about anybody in Russia?
He says, oh, yeah.
I think I heard about it.
In fact, the orchestra leader who's at this affair, he might tell you something about it because he spent time in the Russia too.
So I went over to that orchestra leader, and I asked him if he knew something about.
Now, to bring it out, my sister and I separated during the war, just like I told you in the beginning of my story.
And after the war, I went to Poland and Germany and looked for my sister.
But I never found her.
And I never found any traces of her.
And I didn't know if she was survive the war or she did not survive the war.
And whoever I spoke to, they didn't know nothing about her.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I was under the impression that she did not survive the war.
So mind you, that that was 20 years after the war.
That was in 1960.
In 1960, that's when we got the invitation.
So it was exactly 20 years later after I saw my sister.
So I-- but I spoke to that orchestra leader, and I asked him about if he heard something about my brother-in-law.
And he says, sure.
And the question, this next question was, how about his wife?
And I told him that she was my sister.
Is she alive?
And he says, Sure, she's alive.
And he told me, yeah.
She lives in America.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I found out that my sister is alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the next thing was to find out where she lives.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we did manage-- he didn't know exactly where she was.
But he told me about somebody else, and-- who was in touch with my sister.
And make the long story short, I found out that she lived in Las Vegas.
She remarried, and she lived in Las Vegas.
She came to the United States about 10 years before.
And she remarried.
And she lived in Las Vegas.
It was a hard time for me to get in contact with her.
I found out her address.
I couldn't get her telephone because her telephone was unlisted.
But I managed to find out, and I called her.
But I called her.
She didn't know who I was because she tried to look for me too.
And a lot of people gave her a different leads, bad leads.
So naturally, we-- we found out that we are sisters and brothers because then a couple of months later, a month later I think, she decide and came to Newark to visit me.
And that's-- we had our reunion.
It was all over in the newspaper that brother found his sister after 20 years.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And later on, we decide [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to move to Las Vegas, that I should be closer to-- to my sister.
Pardon me?
No.
No.
My sister never-- didn't have any children.
We came to Las Vegas in 1962.
The first time we came in 1960 for a visit.
And we decided to move to Las Vegas in 1962.
And that's where we moved, to Las Vegas 1962.
Well, I-- my brother-in-law was in business, and he gave me offer to become a partner in his business.
And I took him on the offer.
And here-- and I become a-- a jeweler.
Well, most important of me right now is my family because I-- [DRINKS] I went through so much.
And I seen a lot of families during the war being torn apart, killed, and that's-- my family is my-- most important.
Looking back at my life, I was-- I had like a-- three full lives because my life was divided in the different-- different sections, or different plateaus, or-- or different-- I remember my childhood.
There was my part one part.
Later, the war.
After that was part of my life, and later on, meeting my sister, there was another section-- part of my life.
And now, thank God, I-- I'm OK.
I'm healthy, and maybe comfortable, and having my family together.
I'm married 49 years.
I have one daughter, and I have two lovely grandchildren.
And a son-in-law.
